revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天湯河板挙]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}}}
'''Ame no Yukawatana''' (天湯河桁命) is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|qid=Q178885|lt=kami|1=Deity}} in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|qid=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}} and is also considered an ancient noble of Japan. == Overview == This deity is also referred to as '''Ame no Yukawataka no Mikoto''' (天湯河桁命), '''Ame no Yukawatana no Mikoto''' (天湯川田奈命), and '''Ame no Yukawatana no Kami''' (天湯川田神). The following story is told in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}: The prince {{ill|Homutsuwake no Mikoto|ja|誉津別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homutsuwake no Mikoto|qq=}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|qid=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}}, did not speak even after reaching the age of thirty and instead continued to cry like an infant. One day, upon seeing a [[en:Swan|Swan]], he spoke for the first time, asking, "What is that?" The emperor rejoiced, believing that his son's ability to speak had been awakened by the sight of the bird. He then ordered Ame no Yukawatana to capture the bird. Ame no Yukawatana chased it as far as {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|Izumo Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and according to another source, as far as {{ill|Tajima Province|en|Tajima Province|qid=Q1053727|lt=Tajima Province|1=Tajima Province}}, before successfully capturing the bird.{{Sfn|Nihon Shoki|垂仁天皇23rd year 10月8日条|qq=}} One month later, Ame no Yukawatana presented the captured bird to the emperor. As Prince Homutsuwake played with the bird, he gradually began to speak more. In recognition of his service, the emperor granted Ame no Yukawatana a hereditary title, allowing him to take the name {{ill|Toritori no Miyatsuko|ja|鳥取造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toritori no Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Furthermore, the court established the {{ill|Toritori Be|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toritori Be|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q55525380}}, {{ill|Toriya Be|ja|鳥養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toriya Be|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q55525380}}, and {{ill|Homutsu Be|ja|誉津部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homutsu Be|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{Sfn|Nihon Shoki|垂仁天皇23rd year 11月2日条|qq=}} A similar story appears in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, but the person assigned to capture the bird in that version is {{ill|Yamabe no Ōtori|ja|山辺大鶙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamabe no Ōtori|1=Yamabe no Ōtaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q52319925}}. In that account, he travels across the provinces, sets traps, and undergoes considerable hardship to catch the bird. However, unlike in the Nihon Shoki, the prince's speech disorder is not cured merely by interacting with the bird; rather, a divine revelation in a dream, an omen interpreted by {{ill|Aketatsu no Ōkimi|ja|曙立王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aketatsu no Ōkimi|1=Aketatsu no Ōkimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11515056}}, and the construction of a shrine for {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|qid=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}} all play roles in his eventual recovery. == Interpretations == {{ill|Shinobu Orikuchi|en|Shinobu Orikuchi|qid=Q3482286|lt=Shinobu Orikuchi|1=Shinobu Orikuchi}}, in his work *Fudoki no Kodai Seikatsu* (風土記の古代生活), proposed the theory that the name "Ame no Yukawatana" is derived from "Yukawatana," a sacred place for ritual purification (禊, misogi). He argued that such places were typically located at the confluence of rivers leading to the sea, where warm water (湯, yu) was used for purification. He suggested that the name "Ame no Yukawatana" symbolizes the act of chasing a white bird while seeking a place for ritual cleansing. Meanwhile, {{ill|Yoshida Togo|ja|吉田東伍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Togo|1=Tōgo Yoshida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11413910}} interpreted the name differently. He suggested that "Tana" (桁) was an ancient place name in what is now {{ill|Hinenosho, Osaka|ja|日根郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinenosho, Osaka|1=Hine district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11509546}}. He pointed to a passage in the Nihon Shoki describing a different prince, {{ill|Ikinishiki no Iribiko no Mikoto|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命|1=Prince Inishikiirihiko|2=ja|3=五十瓊敷入彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikinishiki no Iribiko no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Inishikiirihiko|qq=}}, where he is said to have "summoned the Katanagawa upstream" ("鍛名河上を喚して"). Yoshida read this as referring to a location along the "Katagawa River" (桁川).{{Sfn|Nihon Shoki|垂仁天皇39th year 10月条|qq=}} {{ill|Kenichi Tanigawa|ja|谷川健一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenichi Tanigawa|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2392596}} linked these interpretations to metalworking traditions, noting that there are many myths connecting birds and metallurgy. In traditional metal refining, molten metal is called "yu" (湯, hot water), and smiths often sought out specific locations near water for their work. He proposed that Ame no Yukawatana was a figure involved in ancient metalworking and that Prince Homutsuwake's speech impairment may have been caused by {{ill|Mercury poisoning|en|Mercury poisoning|qid=Q408089|lt=mercury poisoning|1=Mercury poisoning}}, an occupational hazard of metal refinement.{{Sfn|Tanigawa|1988|qq=}} == Descendants == According to the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|qid=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}, the Toritori no Miyatsuko clan, who claimed descent from Ame no Yukawatana, belonged to the "Divine Clans" (神別) of the capital: {{quote|鳥取連、角凝魂命三世孫天湯河桁命之後也|qq=}} The clan received the title of "Muraji" (連) in the 12th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign (683 CE).{{Sfn|Nihon Shoki|Emperor Tenmu12nd year 9月23日条|qq=}} The *Shinsen Shōjiroku* also identifies Ame no Yukawatana as the ancestor of the {{ill|Toritori Be|ja|鳥取部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toritori Be|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥養部|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q55525380}}, the {{ill|Minu no Muraji|ja|美努連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minu no Muraji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Minu clan) of {{ill|Yamashiro Province|ja|Yamashiro Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamashiro Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and various branches of the Toritori clan in {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|qid=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}} and {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|qid=Q1047117|lt=Izumi Province|1=Izumi Province}}. There are also several shrines dedicated to him, including the {{ill|Tenyukawatana Shrine|ja|天湯川田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenyukawatana Shrine|1=Amayukawata Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q63891125}} recorded in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|qid=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}} among the shrines of Kawachi Province.{{Sfn|Nihon Shoki|1994|qq=}} == Shrines Dedicated to Ame no Yukawatana == * {{ill|Totoi Shrine|ja|止止井神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Totoi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503304|1=Totoi Shrine}} (Maesawa, Oshu, Iwate) * {{ill|Toritori Shrine (Tōin, Mie)|ja|鳥取神社 (東員町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toritori Shrine (Tōin, Mie)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503306|1=Tottori Shrine (Tōin, Mie)}} (Tōin, Inabe District, Mie) * {{ill|Toritori Shrine (Inabe, Mie)|ja|鳥取神社 (いなべ市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toritori Shrine (Inabe, Mie)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503307|1=Tottori Shrine (Inabe, Mie)}} (Inabe, Mie) * {{ill|Toritori Yamada Shrine|ja|鳥取山田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toritori Yamada Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503309|1=Tottori Yamada Shrine}} (Tōin, Inabe District, Mie) * {{ill|Kawageta Mikawabe Shrine|ja|河桁御河邊神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawageta Mikawabe Shrine|1=Kawaketamikabe Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11553975}} (Higashiomi, Shiga) * {{ill|Kawageta Shrine (Hikone)|ja|川桁神社 (彦根市甲崎町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawageta Shrine (Hikone)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503311|1=Kawageta Shrine (Hikone)}} (Hikone, Shiga) * {{ill|Kawata Shrine (Koka)|ja|川田神社 (甲賀市水口町北内貴)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawata Shrine (Koka)|1=Kawata Shrine (Koka)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11479035}} (Koka, Shiga) * {{ill|Tenyukawatana Shrine|ja|天湯川田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenyukawatana Shrine|1=Amayukawata Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q63891125}} (Kashiwara, Osaka) * {{ill|Hata Shrine (Hannan)|ja|波太神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hata Shrine (Hannan)|1=Hata Shrine (Hannan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q116505726}} (Hannan, Osaka) * {{ill|Mino Agata Shrine|ja|御野縣主神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mino Agata Shrine|1=Mino Agatanushi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11489340}} (Yao, Osaka) * {{ill|Amuka Shrine|ja|阿牟加神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amuka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503313|1=Amuka Shrine}} (Toyooka, Hyōgo) * {{ill|Mino Shrine (Toyooka)|ja|三野神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mino Shrine (Toyooka)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503315|1=Mino Shrine (Toyooka)}} (Toyooka, Hyōgo) * {{ill|Nakajima Shrine|ja|中嶋神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakajima Shrine|1=Nakashima Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11364695}} (Toyooka, Hyōgo) * {{ill|Wanami Shrine|ja|和奈美神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanami Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503316|1=Wanami Shrine}} (Yabu, Hyōgo) == References == {{Reflist|qq=}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=谷川健一|title=白鳥伝説|publisher=集英社文庫|date=1988|qq=}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Toriya Be|ja|鳥養部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toriya Be|1=Torikai-be|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q55525380}} * {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|qid=Q133935|lt=Tottori Prefecture|1=Tottori Prefecture}} * {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|qid=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}}
{{translated page|ja|天湯河板挙|version=93872686|comment=Imported full ja history}}
